title: Chapter 14: Stones and Spitting
paragraphs: Ten winters passed, and Yashil had grown—as much as that word could even be applied to a goblin. He remained small by the standards of any other race: the top of his head barely reached the waist of an average elf. But among his own, he stood out. Not by height—by his eyes. In them burned something that other goblins instinctively avoided, because they could not name it.
Hope. They avoided hope.

By this age, Yashil had already tried to help travelers on the road three times, and three times he had received the same in return: blows, curses, and threats. On his back, a long scar from a hunting knife turned white—a gift from a dwarven prospector whose dropped pouch Yashil had tried to return.

"You are the stupidest goblin I have ever seen," Gnaw, the Hollow's elder, repeated daily, shaking his ugly head. "They will never accept us. They _do not want_ to accept us. Our business is to steal, hide, and survive. It has been thus for a thousand years. It will always be thus."
"A thousand years ago, no one tried any other way," Yashil answered quietly.
Gnaw spat and walked away, muttering curses.
It happened on an evening when the rusty sun touched the horizon, painting the wasteland the color of clotted blood.

Yashil was sitting on the edge of the ravine, looking at the road—his habit for so many years—when he noticed movement in the shadow of a dead baobab. A figure. Big. Very big. Too big for an elf, too thin for a troll.
A demon.

Yashil instinctively pressed himself into the ground, his heart racing. Demons meant death. Zakhan's legions spared no one, and goblins least of all: they were not even enemies to the demons, just refuse accidentally caught underfoot.
But this demon was behaving… strangely.

He stood in the middle of the road, and his massive shoulders—covered in skin as black as pitch with dimly shimmering crimson veins—were slumped. He was not moving. He was standing. In his posture read such a deep, unhuman bewilderment that Yashil involuntarily leaned forward.
And then the demon did something completely incredible.

Carefully, with two clawed hands, he picked up a wounded bird from the road. A small speckled creature with a broken wing. He held it as gently as if it were a crystal vessel, and through his fingers, scattering the twilight, pulsed a faint dark-red glow. The demon was trying to heal it.
Yashil forgot how to breathe.
The bird jerked, chirped—and went still. The glow faded. The demon looked at the small body in his huge palms and made a sound that Yashil did not expect to hear from a creature of the Abyss.
He sobbed.
"Again," the demon said hoarsely, his voice sounding like the noise of breaking coals. "Again, it didn't work. My power only kills. I cannot… I cannot do anything good. Everything I touch dies."
He gently placed the bird at the roots of a tree and covered it with a dry leaf. This gesture—tender, infinitely clumsy—was the most unexpected sight Yashil had seen in his entire life.
The goblin stood to his full, small height and stepped out of the bushes.
"Hello," he said.

The demon spun around with such speed that the air whistled. His eyes—two fires burning red in a skull of black bone—stared at the goblin. Claws involuntarily extended, and a tail with a spiked mace-like tip whipped into a combat position.
Yashil did not flinch. His heart was pounding so hard he feared the demon could hear it. But he did not flinch.
"You were crying," Yashil said. Not asking. Stating.
The demon was silent for an age. Then his claws slowly retracted.
"You are either the bravest goblin in the world," he growled, "or the most insane."
"I am Yashil," the goblin replied and sat right on the road before the creature that could tear him in half with one movement of a claw. "And you?"
The demon looked down at him for a long time. Then—slowly, as if remembering a long-forgotten skill—he lowered himself to the ground beside him.
"Kirah," he said. "My name is Kirah. And I… I do not want to kill."
They sat until dawn. Two outcasts—a small green goblin and a huge black demon—sat side by side on an empty road and, for the first time in their lives, felt they were not alone.
Kirah told his story haltingly, with long pauses, as if each word came through pain.

He was born in Zakhan's legions. From the first day, one truth was hammered into him: you are a weapon. You are created for destruction, and in that is your only value. Weakness is death. Pity is betrayal. Zakhan did not tolerate defectors.

"But I looked at them," Kirah rasped, twirling a broken branch in his claws. "At those we killed. At the villages we burned. They screamed. They called for their loved ones. And every time I thought: 'What if we are wrong? What if killing is not strength, but cowardice?'"
"And what did you do?" Yashil asked.
Kirah went silent. Then he turned his scarred face to him. Half of his left horn was broken off, leaving a jagged, uneven edge.
"I left. At night. Alone. Zakhan does not forgive deserters. If his hunters find me, they will skin me alive and use my bones as ornaments."
"Then why did you leave?"
"Because…" Kirah hesitated, choosing his words, "because staying was worse than dying. I do not want to be a monster, Yashil. I want to be… good. But I do not know how. I couldn't even save a bird."
Yashil looked at the demon's huge, scar-covered hands. Hands created for killing, which had just tried to heal a small bird.
"You are already good, Kirah," the goblin said softly. "You just don't know it yet."
Kirah looked at him with an expression that, on a demonic face, looked completely lost. No one—not once in his existence—had ever said anything like that to him.
"You are a strange goblin," he finally said.
"I'm told that often," Yashil grinned and held out a small green hand.
Kirah looked at it. Then, carefully, barely touching it, he wrapped two clawed fingers around it.
Thus, on the shoulder of a forgotten road, in a world preparing for its end, a friendship was born that should not have existed.
